Resume

About

About

I work across data engineering, analytics, and operations-facing problem solving, with a long-standing interest in infrastructure, planning, and how real systems behave in practice.

Working Style

I usually start by asking which number or workflow people no longer trust.

Most of my work starts the same way: the data exists, but the usable version does not. I like the point where operations, logic, and implementation meet because that is where vague definitions and edge cases finally become concrete.

Start here

definitions first

Bias

real workflows over demos

Default

plain, careful language

Background

My background is one reason this site does not fit neatly into one category.

I studied Data Science and Business Economics at UC San Diego, with a minor in Urban Studies and Planning. That mix left me equally interested in technical systems, incentives, and the institutions those systems sit inside.

It also explains why the work here ranges from warehouse modeling and product measurement to transportation analysis and policy questions without feeling disconnected to me.

Perspective

Transportation and planning still shape the way I think.

Transportation and planning gave me a durable interest in throughput, access, incentives, and the downstream effects of technical choices.

That perspective still shapes why I care about trustworthy metrics, practical fairness, and systems that still hold up in day-to-day use.

Recurring Interests

The same themes keep showing up outside the job titles.

Transit and public infrastructure I keep returning to projects that make transportation systems and planning trade-offs easier to understand.

Fairness and incentives I am interested in where model quality, institutional incentives, and real decision quality start to pull apart.

Small automation I like practical tools that remove recurring friction, even when the workflow is ordinary.

What To Expect

What people can usually expect from my work.

The pattern is usually straightforward. I try to make the problem easier to see, the logic easier to follow, and the handoff easier for the next team.

Start

Fix the definition before the presentation layer.

If people do not trust the source or the logic, a cleaner dashboard will not solve the real problem.

Build

Make the workflow easier for the next team to run.

The goal is not just to ship something useful once. It is to leave behind something that keeps working without constant repair.

Use

Keep the decision context visible.

I care most about work that helps someone make a better call, not just work that produces an interesting output.

In practice

The common thread is simple.

I like work that turns ambiguity into something another person can use. That can look like a warehouse model, a review workflow, a product readout, or a planning case study, but the job underneath is usually the same.

Contact

I am looking for work where clear thinking and careful execution both matter.

If that sounds relevant to your team, you can reach me at tsepaksang8@gmail.com.

Edwin Tse

I build data systems and reporting workflows that make day-to-day work easier to read, trust, and use.

Outside work I keep coming back to transit systems, public infrastructure, fairness questions, and small automation tools that remove repeat friction.
